Commercial Apr 1, 2026

Why the 787-10 uses fewer wheels than the A350-1000

Why In The World Does The Boeing 787-10 Dreamliner Have Fewer Wheels Than The Airbus A350-1000?At first glance, the landing gear of modern widebody aircraft can look almost identical. Large twin-engine jets sit low on the ground, supported by multiple wheels arranged in similar patterns, and unless...

The 787-10's lighter structure and advanced materials allow it to distribute landing loads more efficiently with fewer wheels than the heavier A350-1000, reducing maintenance complexity and weight.

See Simple Flying →

Air Cargo Apr 1, 2026

Why Do Cargo Carriers Still Avoid Twin-Engine Replacements?

Older jets can still be highly valuable if they offer the right mix of range, volume, and operating economics.

Legacy cargo aircraft remain economically competitive due to their favorable depreciation and lower acquisition costs compared to modern twin-engine alternatives with similar payload capabilities.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

After three years, Artemis 2 astronauts ready to launch

Even after three years of public appearances as a crew, there can still be some surprises for the Artemis 2 astronauts.

Artemis 2's three-year delay underscores the complexity of lunar missions and extended preparation timelines that challenge NASA's crewed spaceflight schedules.

Finland's drone incident protocols signal escalating security concerns that may prompt other Nordic nations to establish similar airspace monitoring frameworks.

How The Mass Production Of This Fighter Jet Keeps Its Price Down

With over a thousand F-35s built and thousands more on order, the F-35's flyaway costs can be lower than some fourth-generation fighter jets.

High-volume F-35 production achieves economies of scale that make unit costs competitive with older single-engine fighters despite greater complexity.

Safety Apr 1, 2026

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter...

Pilots systematically underestimate wire strike danger because they overestimate their reaction time, making this a cognitive rather than purely technical safety challenge.

Russian An-26 military transport crashes in Crimea, killing all 29 on board

A Russian military transport aircraft crashed in occupied Crimea on March 31, killing all 29 people on board, according to…...

The crash highlights Russia's reliance on aging Soviet-era transport aircraft, as the An-26 first entered service in 1969 and lacks modern safety systems.

Industry Apr 1, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across integrated airline-airport-flight systems offers immediate environmental and capacity gains before next-generation aircraft technologies mature.

Chinese startup tests flexible robotic arm in space for on-orbit servicing

A Chinese commercial company has conducted an on-orbit demonstration of a flexible robotic arm, marking progress toward capabilities for satellite servicing, refueling and debris removal.

Successful on-orbit robotic arm testing signals that commercial satellite servicing infrastructure is transitioning from concept to operational capability, potentially transforming orbital maintenance economics.

Safety Apr 1, 2026

March of the Harmattan

Strong winds in March 2026 carried Saharan dust across northwestern Africa and toward the Canary Islands, reducing visibility and prompting alerts.

Seasonal dust events like the Harmattan require airlines to monitor visibility forecasts and adjust operations, as African dust plumes can rapidly degrade air quality across European routes.
